data.title: Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the quarter, the exchange-traded fund's net asset value gained 11.17% and its market price advanced 11.25%, topping the 10.51% result of the benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index. U.S. large-cap growth stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April and has been sustained by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reduction since December. In Q3, we continued to focus on companies we believe have above-average earnings-growth potential and a sustainable business model.

The benchmark entered July on an uptrend, gaining 32.76% from April 9 through midyear. The rally was sparked by a 90-day pause on most tariffs announced a week earlier by the Trump administration. Renewed expectations for Fed rate cuts also supported stocks. On September 17, the central bank lowered its federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points, shifting from its wait-and-see stance.

Large-cap growth stocks rose 3.78% in July, buoyed by a massive tax and spending package and President Trump's extension of the deadline for reciprocal tariffs. The index gained another 1.12% in August following news that the U.S. economy grew 3.3% in the second quarter, driven by trade-related momentum, increased business investment and strong consumer spending.

On September 25, Q2 gross domestic product was revised upward to 3.8%, marking the fastest pace since the third quarter of 2023. The Russell 1000® Growth Index advanced 5.31% in September, lifting its year-to-date gain to 17.24%.

In the third quarter, the index was driven by strength in the growth-oriented information technology (+15%) and communication services (+12%) sectors – reflecting the transformative potential of generative artificial intelligence – as well as in consumer discretionary (+10%). These three groups combined represented about 77% of the index for the three months. In contrast, defensive-oriented stocks trailed the broader market, with real estate (-6%) and consumer staples (-4%) losing ground and health care rising 3%.

Stock picking in information technology contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark in Q3, especially among software & services holdings. The top individual relative contributor was an overweight in digital advertising company AppLovin (+105%). The firm is using AI to improve ad targeting on mobile games. Demand for AppLovin's products continued to grow strongly in Q3, as the company expanded its platform to connected TV apps and e-commerce. We reduced exposure in Q3, but AppLovin was a sizable holding and overweight on September 30.

Another notable relative contributor from the tech sector was an overweight in Astera Labs (+117%). The firm specializes in connectivity chips that link components in data center servers, which are critical for AI and cloud infrastructure. Shares of Astera rose sharply in early August after the company reported Q2 financial results. Results were boosted by demand for its fabric switches for AI workloads. Notably, Astera's Q2 revenue was up 150%. As a result, the firm lifted its projections for Q3 earnings and revenue.

Conversely, security selection in consumer discretionary hurt, especially an underweight in electric-vehicle maker Tesla (+40%). We thought Tesla's profit margin would fall as the firm lowered prices on its EVs. But the stock outperformed the past three months, despite a challenging start. The stock fell early in the quarter, as sentiment was clouded by political tension. However, Tesla regained momentum late in the quarter, supported by CEO Elon Musk's $1 billion purchase of company stock in the open market.

Also in consumer discretionary, a large overweight in Amazon.com (0%) detracted for the three months. Concerns about a slowdown in Amazon's cloud division and perceived lag in AI innovation weighed on the stock. We trimmed to the fund's position, but Amazon ended the quarter as the No. 4 position and top overweight. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As Q4 begins, we are cautiously optimistic that the stock market will climb a "wall of worry" in the next 12 months. Accordingly, we are closely monitoring what we consider a chaotic macro environment, with tariffs, interest rates, inflation, global conflict and the potential for a recession all posing as headwinds to stocks.

On the valuation front, at quarter end the market is trading higher than our estimation of fair value. Still, we've found investment opportunities among certain segments of the market that have anticipated a recession or have undergone an inventory correction when valuations look attractive. The fund has exposure to some of these cyclical segments of the market, such as semiconductors and consumer discretionary, where we believe earnings estimates have largely already been reset. Should an economic slowdown materialize, and the Fed cut interest rates further, we believe growth stocks will be well-positioned to benefit. We plan to continue to closely monitor the situation and position the fund accordingly.

We continue to look for signs that the macro environment is becoming more predictable, and inflation remains contained. We are favoring companies that have pricing power and can effectively navigate this turbulent environment. We remain interested in businesses that are capable of growth, regardless of the environment. We also look for companies that may be perceived as being "down and out," but have turnaround potential and that may boost the fund's performance in the next 12 to 24 months. Turnarounds are often accompanied by a change in management team or a strong product cycle that may accelerate revenue growth and improve profitability.

The fund's largest sector overweights as of September 30 are communication services and consumer discretionary.

In consumer discretionary, e-commerce leader Amazon.com is the fund's fourth-largest holding and biggest overweight. We think Amazon is strategically positioned to capitalize on the secular tailwind of increasing penetration in global e-commerce.

The fund holds positions to capitalize on a housing cycle that we think is likely to improve due to strong pent-up demand for housing and lower interest rates, which should help with affordability. The fund holds non-benchmark positions in home improvement retailer Lowe's (LOW) and furnishing-related companies RH (RH) and Wayfair (W), as we believe each stock is trading at a depressed valuation. We believe all are poised to benefit when housing turnover improves.

The fund also has exposure to apparel companies in the athleisure and sneakers categories, as consumers around the globe increasingly prioritize health, fitness and adoption of athleisure wear. As tariff-related volatility subsides, these companies should demonstrate improved business fundamentals, in our view. It should be noted that we selectively trimmed some positions in this group, due to deteriorating company-specific fundamentals.

Although information technology was the fund's biggest sector underweight this quarter, AI remains an important secular growth trend and driver of market returns. Despite macro uncertainty, we remain confident in this trend long term, and we have started to see improved returns on investment for cloud-service providers that have fueled much of the spending on AI. We will continue to scour the market for companies using AI in innovative ways to drive earnings growth.

The fund has notable overweights in semiconductor makers Nvidia and Marvell Technology. Nvidia is the leading supplier of systems used for generative AI models. Marvell enables several cloud players to develop internal chips for generative AI. Nvidia was the fund's largest holding, while Marvell is No. 12, even though we modestly reduced it in Q3. ■

Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF (FBCG)

This ETF is different from traditional ETFs. Traditional ETFs tell the public what assets they hold each day. This ETF will not. This may create additional risks for your investment. For example: You may have to pay more money to trade the ETF's shares. This ETF will provide less information to traders, who tend to charge more for trades when they have less information. The price you pay to buy ETF shares on an exchange may not match the value of the ETF's portfolio. The same is true when you sell shares. These price differences may be greater for this ETF compared to other ETFs because it provides less information to traders. These additional risks may be even greater in bad or uncertain market conditions. The ETF will publish on its website each day a "Tracking Basket" designed to help trading in shares of the ETF. While the Tracking Basket includes some of the ETF's holdings, it is not the ETF's actual portfolio. The differences between this ETF and other ETFs may also have advantages. By keeping certain information about the ETF secret, this ETF may face less risk that other traders can predict or copy its investment strategy. This may improve the ETF's performance. If other traders are able to copy or predict the ETF's investment strategy, however, this may hurt the ETF's performance. For additional information regarding the unique attributes and risks of this ETF, see Fund Risks in the Definitions and Important Information section.

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Blue Chip Growth ETF is a domestic equity growth strategy with a large-cap bias.

Our investment approach focuses on companies we believe have above-average earnings-growth potential with a sustainable business model, for which the market has mispriced the rate and/or durability of growth.

In particular, we look for events that might provide a business catalyst – such as product cycles, a change in management and turnaround situations – that could add to a stock's true value. We believe choosing companies with a competitive advantage, pricing power and a strong management team can deliver superior earnings in the long term.

We look to capitalize on inappropriate valuations in the market through bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.

Characteristics

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Fund Risks

The objective of the actively managed ETF Tracking Basket is to construct a portfolio of stocks and representative index ETFs that tracks the daily performance of an actively managed ETF without

exposing current holdings, trading activities, or internal equity research. The Tracking Basket is designed to conceal any nonpublic information about the underlying portfolio and only uses the Fund's latest publicly disclosed holdings, representative ETFs, and the publicly known daily performance in its construction. Although the Tracking Basket is intended to provide investors with enough information to allow for an effective arbitrage mechanism that will keep the market price of the Fund at or close to the underlying NAV per share of the Fund, there is a risk (which may increase during periods of market disruption or volatility) that market prices will vary significantly from the underlying NAV of the Fund; ETFs trading on the basis of a published Tracking Basket may trade at a wider bid/ask spread than ETFs that publish their portfolios on a daily basis, especially during periods of market disruption or volatility, and, therefore, may cost investors more to trade, and although the Fund seeks to benefit from keeping its portfolio information secret, market participants may attempt to use the Tracking Basket to identify a Fund's trading strategy, which, if successful, could result in such market participants engaging in certain predatory trading practices that may have the potential to harm the Fund and its shareholders. Because shares are traded in the secondary market, a broker may charge a commission to execute a transaction in shares, and an investor may incur the cost of the spread between the price at which a dealer will buy shares and the price at which a dealer will sell shares. The fund may have a limited number of financial institutions that act as authorized participants, none of which are obligated to engage in creation and/or redemption transactions. To the extent that those authorized participants do not engage in creation and redemption orders, there may be a significantly diminished trading market for fund shares or fund shares may trade at a discount (or premium) to NAV and possibly face trading halts and/or de-listing. Unlike mutual funds, ETF shares are bought and sold at market price, which may be higher or lower than their NAV, and are not individually redeemed from the fund. Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. "Growth" stocks can react differently to issuer, political, market, and economic developments than the market as a whole and other types of stocks. The fund may have additional volatility because it can invest a significant portion of assets in securities of a small number of individual issuers.

Russell 1000 Growth Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the US equity market. Trading Information

Bid/Ask Spread

In the case of exchange-traded securities, market liquidity is managed by the market maker community. Market makers provide prices at which they will buy (the bid) and sell (the ask) securities. The difference between the bid and ask prices is the bid/ask spread. The bid/ask spread gives an investor a sense of the liquidity and the transaction costs associated with a security. Securities with wide bid/ask spreads tend to be less liquid and

have higher cost of ownership. Those with tight bid/ask spreads tend to be more liquid and have a lower cost of ownership.

Premium/Discount

The exchange-quoted price of an ETF frequently differs from the ETF's underlying value. The difference in these values represents an ETF's discount or premium. In the case of a discount, the exchange-quoted share price is lower than the ETF's underlying value. An ETF is at a premium when the exchange-quoted price is greater than its underlying value. An ETF's discount or premium fluctuates over time and is influenced by such factors as the transaction costs of the underlying holdings, the cost of creation and redemption of shares, and the average daily volume. The magnitude and volatility of an ETF's premium/discount will affect an investor's holding period return on an ETF. If an investor buys shares at a discount and sells at a premium, they should outperform the NAV return of the ETF. On the other hand, if an investor buys at a premium and sells at a discount, they should underperform the NAV return of the ETF.

3-Year Risk/Return Statistics

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Will Market Uncertainty Continue In 2026?
data.description:
Originally published on December 11, 2025

Markets have been continuing their upward momentum as inflation pressures ease and rate cuts take shape. But stretched valuations and tariff uncertainty are keeping investors on edge. Jeff Evans, Vice President, Director and Lead of Empirical Research and PM Support with TD Asset Management, discusses the current state of the markets and how ETFs can help investors diversify during periods of uncertainty.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Let's start with the big picture. And this is probably one of these times-- it's always like this. There's just so many undercurrents in the market, the big picture doesn't probably properly reflect what's going on. But what do you see?

Jeff Evans: So, I'd say, first of all, we've come a long way from 2022. If you just go back a couple of years ago, we had the biggest increase and the fastest increase in interest rates in 40 years, driven by the biggest increase in inflation in 40 years, driven by, arguably, the biggest monetary and fiscal stimulus ever. So there was a lot going on in 2022. The risk at the time was, can central banks bring inflation under control fast enough without breaking the economy?

Lots of people worried that you'd have to keep interest rates elevated, and ultimately break the labor market and break the overall economy. The good news-- here we are two, three years later, it looks like they've mostly pulled that off. We've been able to bring inflation not all the way to 2, but into a much more comfortable level. The glide path into '26 and '27 looks like we should get back on track fairly quickly.

And more importantly, we've been able to bring inflation under control without hurting the labor market-- which, historically, is very unusual. Normally, there'd be a lot more unemployment than what we saw in this cycle. And even more interesting, unemployment rates have gone up in a lot of major developed market economies-- Canada from 5 and 1/2 to 7, for example. But it's not really because people are losing their jobs. We're actually still creating new jobs.

The issue is we've had a lot of immigration, and there's just been a challenge of finding new jobs for everybody who's come into the economies. And so the challenge here is with high interest rates, high inflation, it's going to stall that rate of job creation. With interest rates back to a neutral level, hopefully we can keep the labor market intact. And then when you start thinking about 2026, a lot of the benefits from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that was passed in the summer, they don't really kick in until next year. And I think that's maybe a little bit misunderstood.

The bill is passed. Everything is authorized and approved. And then you get into next year, and around April you're going to start to see a lot bigger tax refunds than people perhaps are expecting. Companies start to get a lot of rebates on their CapEx. And so that's going to be very helpful from an economic stimulus perspective next year. I think the more interesting question is going to be-- the economy looks like it's flying, is the equity market maybe getting a little too ahead of it?

So I think the way to think about the market environment right now is, the economy should be fine, we've pushed a lot of the downside risks out a few years. And then the question is, have the equity markets really gotten perhaps a little ahead of the curve on pricing these things in?

Kim Parlee: Can you answer that question?

Jeff Evans: Well, I wish I could. And I think the best answer to that is, we know corrections happen. That's a normal part of the equity markets. We don't know when and we don't know by how much. And so that's the challenge. If you were to look at things like broader equity market valuations, the S&P 500, we're starting to hear the "bubble" word tossed around by a few people. S&P 500 is trading at about 22 times earnings today. That's nowhere near the 40 times that you saw in the dot-com bubble, we're well below those levels. But if you exclude the dot-com bubble, 22 times is basically the highest valuations that you'd seen outside of that time period. So markets are certainly pricing in high expectations.

And if you look at earnings growth expectations in the market right now, just what is consensus pricing in? We expect-- the typical long-term average earnings growth is around 6% for the S&P 500. Consensus is looking for 15% each year for the next two years, so double, maybe even triple the normal rate of earnings growth.

As long as companies deliver, everything's fine. These valuations are defendable if that earnings growth materializes and if companies meet expectations. And so far this year, they have-- every quarter that we've seen, and particularly the last two or three, meeting expectations or even giving increased guidance. So the tone from companies is becoming fairly positive. But that means the bar gets higher. It gets a little bit harder to beat every time.

And we just don't know. We don't know if at some point in '26 or '27, you start to see companies have maybe gone a little too far, a little too optimistic-- and that will be the big risk for markets, is if you do start to see a slowdown and companies can't beat these very lofty expectations.

Kim Parlee: I guess, so what do you do? So again, no one can tell when the next downturn happens. No one can tell the magnitude of what's going to happen. And it sounds a bit like a motherhood statement to say diversify, diversify. But it's true. In the long term, that is the only thing that can guard against these types of things in the long term.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. And I think everybody thinks they can sell at the top and buy in at the bottom and market-time. And there are a handful of people that have done that successfully over time. But for the vast majority of investors, having a diversified portfolio is what helps you. When you're going through these periods, you're not going to be caught off guard. You've got one side of the portfolio maybe going down, and another side is stable or going up in an ideal scenario.

And it gives you the ability, then, if markets are off 20% on the equity side, if you can sell down some fixed income and rotate, take advantage. You don't have that if you go in at 100% equities into these. So making sure you have that properly structured, diversified portfolio really helps out in these periods to then take advantage of that dislocation.

If you look at the last 25 years, probably the best way to diversify a portfolio is, if you're starting with equities, is to add bonds. The returns are a little bit lower, but they are very uncorrelated with the broader equity market, a lot less volatile. And what we've seen is quite often when equities are selling off, bonds are actually rallying and going up in price. So that is a great diversifier.

But the other option, if you're looking at just the equity sleeve and the portfolio, is to look for sectors or strategies that are uncorrelated with the broader equity markets-- which is, increasingly, large-cap tech.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, and I think I was even going to pose the question, because I know people who may be a bit younger than me, they've been on the wave of the Mag 7, have done really, really well. But it's like-- and now what? And so when you say diversify, not everything has to happen right at once. There might be things that might be more interesting at this particular time, from a diversification standpoint, than others. So, what would those be?

Jeff Evans: So the few that we've been flagging-- and it's a trend that was going on for quite a while within the Canadian pension funds and a lot of global pension funds-- is moving away from equities and bonds and adding real assets. So we've started to see, particularly the big Canadian pension funds have 20-30% of their portfolios allocated to things like real estate and infrastructure. A big part of that is, they are only moderately correlated with broader equity markets, very little correlation from real estate to big tech.

We're seeing a little more on the infrastructure side because, of course, as AI starts to become more prominent, you're actually starting to impact utility companies. A lot of these are in some ways a derivative play of AI, so you have to be a little careful. But what we've seen over the course of this year, when markets sold off earlier around March and April, they were down 20%. Infrastructure was basically flat, real estate down maybe 5-6%. And so you get a great diversification benefit in these periods of market turmoil. So I'd say those are probably the two best sectors.

Kim Parlee: And I was going to say, on those sectors, just full disclosure, you're involved with a couple of ETFs at TDAM that do this.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. We have a few products, actively managed solutions. One of those products, TGRE:CA, has been around. In fact, both of them have been around for five years. TINF:CA is our infrastructure solution. And what's unique about that is, one, we have a global focus, so we're not just focusing on Canada or US. It does give you a truly global product. It allows us to move around if one part of the market is becoming more concentrated.

And then, what's also helpful here is that with an active approach, we can move our exposures around over time. If we're starting to see certain sectors slow down or earnings growth deteriorate, we have the flexibility to adjust exposures. And historically, that's also tended to help in downturns. We can pivot a little more defensively and try to protect some of the portfolio value.

Kim Parlee: I know you were speaking earlier with my producer, actually. We're talking about another interesting thing to think about at this time, specifically, is just in the long term-- I'm going to paraphrase this and you tell me if it's right or wrong-- that, given where rates are going, they've been low for longer and now they're probably going to be higher. And then, which companies can withstand that? Who have the strong balance sheets? Who have the strong dividends, the strong yields? And that's a good place to look in the long term.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. And we've come from an interesting period. Lots of things are changing in markets. If you look at 2010s, capital was basically free. You could borrow money in the debt markets for very low interest rates. Generally, companies get access to equity if they need it to grow. It's changed a little bit in the last two or three years. With, interest rates and inflation, there's a cost of capital again. And that means, if your company is growing aggressively and you need capital, if that capital is not there-- the debt market shut down, or if the interest cost is too expensive, or you can't raise equity-- there's always a risk in those companies that they need funds at the wrong time and they can't access it.

When you look at these environments where interest rates are perhaps a little bit higher than they have been in the past, companies that can self-fund usually are far more resilient than companies that are dependent on external financing. Usually, that comes from being profitable. And if you're profitable, usually, you have the ability to pay a dividend-- and then, more importantly, to grow that dividend over time.

Dividend investing over the long term has generally outperformed markets by a couple percentage points a year. Not so much last decade, because circumstances changed. But we're starting to see that come back in now, where investors are focusing, again, on yield because interest rates have come off the peak from two years ago. But just in general, it's about having resilient companies that, instead of consuming capital, can actually give it back to you. And the track record over decades tends to suggest that dividend stocks and then dividend strategies usually outperform.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, and I should just mention TQCD:CA is one that TDAM-- an ETF that follows that philosophy.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. It's one that focuses on dividends and also uses a number of different screens to help select within that universe to make sure we're avoiding high payout ratios and so on. So those sorts of strategies are excellent ways of adding some of that exposure to the portfolio.

Kim Parlee: Jeff, great to have you with us. And a reminder just for people who are listening, also, do your own research. And of course, make sure these things fit for you in terms of what doing.

Jeff, thanks so much.

Jeff Evans: Thank you very much.

Original Post

data.title: Microsoft: The Real AI Winner Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Microsoft: Debunking The Pessimism For 2026

A correction engulfing the stock of Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is nothing new, even for the software behemoth. Wait, is Microsoft a software company, or is it a hyperscaler? Should investors consider its AI infrastructure thesis as the second largest cloud computing player by market share more profoundly than the stranglehold that MSFT exercises over its painstakingly built ecosystem in software? I guess that has once again bifurcated investor sentiments once more, akin to the period of underperformance that the stock encountered in early 2025.

However, an attempt to pigeonhole the Redmond company into such distinct categories seems to gloss over what is, after all, a company that has incredibly robust competitive advantages across the tech ecosystem. Makes sense?

  • Microsoft's workloads are deeply embedded in the enterprise value chain, underpinning the incredibly sticky software layer that arguably defines its moat source.
  • It has also broadened its ecosystem to the cloud infra and platform layer, modernizing its edge against peers seeking to dethrone it, but to no avail.
  • Recognizing the inherent potential of OpenAI early in the AI transformation, highlighting the profound vision demonstrated by the Satya Nadella-led company.
  • And, while OpenAI has seen its valuation surge and balloon over the past year, Microsoft stands ready to reap financial gains from that collaboration, with Azure customers gaining tightly-knitted access through Microsoft's links with OpenAI, as AI workloads become increasingly pervasive over time.

Microsoft's Unique Position In Tech

Nevertheless, it does seem like the market has taken a step back to reassess whether MSFT's moat source has indeed stayed “impregnable” amidst the AI onslaught by the AI models or LLM companies, so to speak. After all, if software companies are clearly haunted by the per-seat dilemma that has underpinned the monetization thesis for SaaS's underlying predictability, the new era of compute-based variability could truly upend the previous (or even the current) order and herald a truly ground-shaking paradigm shift that might also disrupt such known business models.

In that regard, I think it's fortuitous that Microsoft has such immense control across the tech stack (hardware, software, and platform). I think Nadella recognizes the immense advantages and leadership that it could confer on the company as it works harder to execute its AGI ambitions.

However, there's little doubt that the market appears reticent in adding further fuel to MSFT's bullish narrative, although a broader reading of the tea leaves should also inform us that a nuanced interpretation is necessary amid the wider tech pullback from its 2025 highs. Yet, it also offers (us, tech investors) a solid opportunity to consider adding to our MSFT conviction, even as the market turns to face away from what could be a truly remarkable year for software companies to regain their clout and mojo, as the great rotation trade plausibly shifts from AI infra picks and shovels plays into the companies that are well-primed to monetize them after all.

Recall that Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) is now (obviously) receiving many plaudits in view of its reemergence from an AI laggard to becoming a feared, cutting-edge competitor owning the so-called full stack. Parlaying the advances deep into the AI value chain now sees Gemini AI getting even "better at connecting with Google Drive for tasks like summarizing the contents of photos stored in folders." In contrast, Microsoft Copilot appears to "for [the] most part don’t really work, and are not smart." That could have ramifications on how the market views MSFT's thesis, right? After all, it's not as if MSFT is valued at a discount (compared to big tech peers), is it?

Azure Could See A Revival As AI Monetization Gains Traction

I think this is where the market is downplaying Microsoft's capabilities across the stack. While it may not quite have the pedigree or prominence of Google's AI custom chip capabilities, it doesn't mean that Microsoft doesn't possess the required systems-level framework to actualize such ambitions. Azure's monetization and growth metrics demonstrate the immense potential that's lurking behind the scenes, as AI could prove to be a propellant to lift buying sentiments on MSFT yet again when 2026 comes into play:

Now Microsoft’s investments in AI are back, and the AI giant has never had such high demand for Accelerated Computing. The Redmond titan has woken up to it going down the wrong path and has dramatically shifted course. With the newly announced OpenAI deal, Azure growth is set to Accelerate in the upcoming quarters as forecasted by our Tokenomics model. Microsoft plays in every single part of the AI Token Economic Stack, is witnessing accelerated growth, and we expect the trend to continue in coming quarters and years. The firm is actively looking for near-term capacity and pulling the trigger on everything it can get its hands on. Self-build, leasing, Neocloud, middle-of-nowhere locations – everything is on the table to accelerate near-term capacity growth. - Semianalysis article on Microsoft's AI strategy

And recent surveys show that Microsoft still commands a leadership position among peers, lowering the possibility that it could cede market share to enterprising rivals in the near term, whether they emanate from the LLM companies or Magnificent rivals like Google.

In addition, Nvidia's recent decision to restructure DGX cloud (NVDA) shows just how challenging it is to untether dominant cloud computing workloads, despite us being in a multicloud environment. And the advantages bestowed upon MSFT's product strategies should still help confer it opportunities to maintain that bifurcation over its closest rivals seeking to unhinge its advantages.

Hence, if enterprise AI adoption were to become more prevalent through 2027 as the monetization narrative for enterprise firms percolates from the experimentation phase to the production phase, then it should conceivably strengthen MSFT's bullish narrative amidst the confusion right now befuddling the software skeptics about Microsoft's unique positional strengths.

Microsoft Isn't Sacrificing Margins To Outcompete

Therefore, I think it doesn't make sense for us to suddenly turn so downcast on MSFT. The company is also astutely leveraging on collaborations with neoclouds to "offload" plausibly more volatile/less profitable training workloads, while retaining the increasingly more valuable inference prospects, which should benefit more profoundly as enterprise AI adoption proliferates through the end of the decade.

In this case, I believe Wall Street's prognosis of seeing Microsoft's CapEx starting to decelerate through FY2028 as credible, supporting the thesis that Microsoft isn't forsaking capital allocation and margin discipline. Such a narrative should help to defuse simmering tensions about Microsoft's AI edge, while allowing the company more time to regain the confidence of investors in its margin predictability and stability.

Buy Or Sell MSFT Stock?

With the stock still valued reasonably with a forward earnings multiple of just above 29x (compared to its 10Y average of 28x), it's in line with the resurgent GOOGL multiple.

Obviously, the market is seeing Google's AI thesis being revalued significantly, but note that Google's ad-heavy revenue model isn't projected to post margins anywhere close to Microsoft's, with estimated FCF margins of just over 17% by FY2027, a discernible disconnect against that of Microsoft's metric.

I'm also seeing more robust buying sentiments underpinning the MSFT recovery narrative right now, and I believe buyers are smartly accumulating while the market looks away to the great rotation trade on other sectors currently.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Sanofi-Dynavax: A Conservative Vaccine Deal With Upside Tail Risk
data.description:
Overview

Dynavax (DVAX) is joining forces with Sanofi (SNY) this Festivus season! Revealed on Christmas Eve, Sanofi will acquire Dynavax for $15.50 per share in cash via a tender offer. This values Dynavax at ~$2.2 billion equity value (roughly 6.9x equity value to HEPLISAV-B revenue before considering Dynavax's net cash). The deal is expected to close next quarter.

Having recently covered both stocks, I thought I’d take a closer look at the strategic rationale.

My last article on Dynavax was in May 2024. The small-cap pharma company was marketing HEPLISAV-B, a hepatitis B vaccine that requires only two doses in one month. Recall that legacy hepatitis B vaccines typically require three doses over six months. The hepatitis B vaccine market is smaller than other vaccine markets, like influenza. So, I figured that HEPLISAV-B’s peak annual revenues would likely top out at ~$600 million. Meanwhile, Dynavax was also advancing a shingles and Tdap vaccine in Phase 1/2 trials. Notably, Dynavax has since axed its Tdap vaccine. I believed that DVAX was appropriately priced and rated it a Hold.

My last coverage of its acquirer, Sanofi, was this October. Thanks to the strength of its immunology blockbuster, DUPIXENT, I noted that Sanofi is flush with cash. Of course, Sanofi is best known for its vaccine division (influenza, RSV), which always serves as a nice bedrock for its higher-margin pharma ambitions. I rated SNY a Buy owing to its DUPIXENT-driven growth, conservative valuation, and friendly dividend yield.

Sanofi's Low-Drama Bolt-On

The Dynavax acquisition is quintessential “playing it safe” for the vaccine giant. Sanofi is acquiring a commercial vaccine that is already generating material revenue. In Q3 2025, HEPLISAV-B generated $90 million in net product revenue (up 13% Y/Y), and Dynavax reiterated its FY25 revenue guidance of between $315 million and $325 million. Certainly, the stronger field footprint, payer contracting leverage, and systems-level adult immunization relationships of Sanofi could take HEPLISAV-B to the “next level,” creeping closer to my peak annual sales estimates before its expiry. Recall that Dynavax expected the US adult hep B market to peak above $900 million by 2030, with HEPLISAV-B reaching at least 60% share.

Given that the consideration is all cash and funded with available cash resources, Sanofi stated no impact on its 2025 financial guidance. So, this is clearly a low-risk, tuck-in acquisition for the French pharma giant.

Sanofi can certainly afford Dynavax. Per Seeking Alpha, in the trailing twelve months, the company generated ~$13 billion in cash from operations. As of the last report, Sanofi carried roughly $10.5 billion in total cash and short-term investments and owed $9.63 billion and $13.7 billion in short- and long-term debt, respectively.

Of course, Sanofi is getting Dynavax’s pipeline as well. Dynavax was planning to reveal complete Phase 1/2 data for its shingles vaccine candidate, Z-1018, in the second half of 2026. This one hopes to show a comparable immune response and better safety profile than GSK’s (GSK) SHINGRIX. Dynavax is also evaluating plague, H5N1, and Lyme disease vaccines.

Taking a step back, Sanofi has been emphasizing vaccines as a growth driver and actively building vaccine assets and partnerships. In 2024, Sanofi entered a co-exclusive licensing agreement with Novavax (NVAX) to co-commercialize a COVID-19 vaccine and develop flu-COVID combinations. In addition, Sanofi has been busy scaling BEYFORTUS supply ahead of RSV seasons.

At its core, Dynavax strengthens Sanofi’s leadership in adult vaccinations and helps diversify its revenue outside of DUPIXENT.

Dynavax's Cash Out: Premium Today, Upside Handed Off

As for Dynavax, the Sanofi acquisition represents a decent exit. The ~39% premium is typical in pharma M&A, and I think the price is pretty generous. While HEPLISAV-B is a nice tuck-in product, it has limited TAM. Meanwhile, product candidates like Z-1018 are uncertain and, at least, remain years away from generating meaningful revenue.

As evidenced by its stock performance in the past 10 years, Dynavax’s management has had a challenging time creating shareholder value.

In fact, Dynavax's underperformance inspired an activist challenge (Deep Track) earlier this year.

Moreover, the company was merely straddling profitability.

Dynavax was a poster child of the challenge associated with building a company around just one small product. Dynavax has been around for nearly three decades and has only HEPLISAV-B and CpG 1018 (adjuvant) to show for it. So, it makes sense that it would eventually dissolve into a more capable pharma company.

Should the deal turn sideways, which I think is highly unlikely, Dynavax will go back to “business as usual.” As of September 30, the company had a healthy $647.8 million in total cash and short-term investments to continue marketing HEPLISAV-B and advance its pipeline candidates. Of course, in the event the deal falls through, Dynavax’s stock would instantly lose the acquisition premium. Moreover, its current price of $15.38, relative to the $15.50 cash consideration, does not offer any meaningful “arbitrage situation” for investors on the sidelines.

Final Words

To close, the Dynavax acquisition is Sanofi choosing “certainty” over “heroics.” It purchases a real product with real revenue that fits in perfectly with its existing portfolio. The shingles vaccine candidate, alongside others, acts as a long-dated call option.

On the other hand, Dynavax gets a fair exit for a company that never proved it could compound shareholder value around a narrow commercial base.

From here, the only real debate is how much incremental value Sanofi can unlock through contracting and channel reach. If it can squeeze HEPLISAV-B to its last drop, the deal works for Sanofi regardless of how Dynavax’s early-stage candidates perform.

From my perspective, in the aftermath of the deal, little has changed for either company (maintain “Hold” on DVAX; “Buy” on SNY). This, too, speaks to the strategic fit of the deal.

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: You Haven't Seen Anything Yet
data.description:
My Previous Takes On SBSW And Updated Thesis

My first research articles on Sibanye Stillwater (SBSW) stock here on Seeking Alpha date back to 2021, when one could buy the stock for a higher price than what SBSW is trading at right now. I initially timed it wrong, but I've been consistently arguing for an eventual comeback when the PGM prices normalize and the side non-PGM projects of Sibanye lead to fruition, which I thought would come soon. Well, we had to wait for longer than I initially assumed, but the comeback came indeed:

Since my most recent bullish call in September 2025, SBSW's total return is over 84%, which is 10x the return of the S&P 500 index (SPX) (SP500), so I'm happy I stayed bullish.

But I think that when it comes to the true potential in SBSW, we've hardly seen anything yet. In other words, SBSW remains too undervalued when we try to forecast the future operating results that should come from continued margin expansion and the business growth itself. I believe the rally isn't over, and that's why SBSW remains a "Buy" for me, even at today's elevated price levels.

My Updated Reasoning On SBSW

Since my September article, SBSW managed to release a very important operating update, which you can find here, among other files. What immediately stood out to me was their EBITDA figure of ~$560 million, which was 3x more than last year. This is what can be called a magnitude of recovery that suggests the company has successfully navigated the trough of the cycle, in my view. And it's not just about adjusted EBITDA. SBSW's cash flow generation has finally accelerated, and it seems to be taking place at a faster pace than the market was initially pricing in. Otherwise, the stock wouldn't add over 50% alone since the November update:

The South African PGM operations turned into a cash machine, with adjusted EBITDA rising by 213% YoY thanks to a more favorable pricing environment. We see that the average 4E basket price increased by 36% (to $1,839/4Eoz), so the 4% increase in total production (to 493,863 4Eoz) over the same period couldn't help but lead to the massive expansion. The rise in underground production (+8%) helped them offset the 31% decline in surface operations that took place due to the depletion of the Waterval West Tailings Storage Facility.

On the side of SBSW's South African Gold Operations segment, we saw the 12th quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA, and particularly in Q3, this metric increased by 177% to $212 million. Again, it wasn't just a price-driven increase because both Driefontein and Beatrix mines showed significant recovery (Driefontein's production alone was up 21% YoY). Yes, the Kloof operation again faced structural challenges, but the net-net effect on the consolidated bottom line was positive.

The US segments' adjusted EBITDA—both US PGM and US Recycling—showed positive numbers ($33 million and ~$15 million, respectively). I liked that the US PGM operations stopped generating deep losses and achieved a positive AISC margin of US$19/2Eoz, which is already something. At the same time, US Recycling's quality of earnings got better, which can be seen through the EBITDA expansion of ~87% YoY for the period, thanks to its strategic shift toward higher-margin industrial scrap processing.

The guidance for FY2025 remains largely unchanged, but there was a specific positive update regarding the US PGM operations. The release noted that this segment should perform better than it was initially forecasted (amid flat production), and the all-in sustaining costs (AISC) should come at the lower end of the previous guidance range, which works well for my bullish thesis on SBSW's prospective margin gains.

The success of the US PGM operations turnaround (at least it looks like a real turnaround to me at this point) is what's driving SBSW higher.

This business line used to be one of the primary overhangs on the stock for months, and now the bleeding has stopped, and things are clearing up fast. Now we can see that SBSW's restructuring efforts from late 2024 have helped lower the breakeven point. Yes, the Q3 margin of just ~2% looks insignificant at first glance, but it's just the beginning, in my opinion. Next year, if the dynamic of the 2H 2025 keeps going, the actual EBITDA from this segment should look pretty solid compared to the full FY2025. In other words, SBSW's positive earnings in the US PGM business unit, even on a relatively small scale compared to other segments, will likely surprise the market in a bullish way.

What can also change next year for SBSW is a potential repricing of the firm's battery metals portfolio. Investors have been skeptical of the company's execution associated with its battery metals pivot (specifically the losses at the Sandouville nickel refinery in France). But once Sandouville's care and maintenance process ends, and it starts to contribute to the consolidated EBITDA (in Q3, it incurred a loss of $7 million), I think it'll become a nice addition to the margins. Also, the firm is looking forward to completing the construction of the Keliber lithium project in Finland in H1 2026. They've already invested €645 million (within the budget), so by mid-2026, if all goes according to the management's plan, we should see SBSW's profitable entry into the battery metals supply chain. If it happens, the stock's multiples should naturally rise because, in my view, the market is currently assigning near-zero value to their battery metals portfolio.

The potential for repricing also lies in consensus data. Based on Seeking Alpha data, there's only one analyst who has provided an estimate for 2027, and the estimate itself doesn't seem to account for the progress made in 2025 (or its longevity).

If we put a fair 9-10x P/E multiple on FY2026 EPS, assuming that FY2027 would mark a slight YoY growth as well, and also assume a 5-10% premium on top of today's forward EPS consensus (for FY2026), we'd get a price target of ~$23.18/sh. That's another 53-54% of the potential rally's continuation, so it's logical from my end to reiterate a "Buy" on SBSW today.

Risks

I should admit that my bullish thesis on SBSW will work only if PGM metals stay relatively strong. But in case we see a severe global recession, or anything of this kind, that crushes automotive demand, and so effectively, SBSW's margins would compress quickly.

Another risk, which has always been relevant for SBSW, is South African operations. In addition to what everyone's aware of—the country risks—there are also some infrastructure challenges there (specifically regarding Eskom and logistics) that threaten the national grid and state logistics. In Q3, the bright example of how this risk can materialize came from SBSW's chrome sales, which dropped by 26% YoY purely because there were port constraints.

Your Takeaway

The risks are high for SBSW, but I believe the rewards outweigh the risks for investors, even at today's prices after a massive 330%+ run-up over the past 12 months. Figuratively speaking, the leaky buckets that used to drain cash flow before—specifically the US PGM losses and the Sandouville refinery—have effectively been fixed (or are about to get fixed shortly). And my valuation model, under realistic assumptions, suggests that SBSW can add 50%+ to its market cap over the next 12 months. That's why I'm still bullish on it.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PG&E: An Undervalued Utility
data.description:
PG&E Corp. (PCG) is a $35 billion utility unit that has been consistently punished by PG&E's history of mismanagement and wildfires. Despite that, the company is working hard to reorient itself. That is positioning the company well for consistent EPS growth and, as we'll see throughout this article, will enable strong shareholder returns for those who invest now.

PG&E Growth

PG&E had $0.50 in non-GAAP core EPS in 3Q 2025, or $1.14 YTD, on track for the company.

PG&E has narrowed its guidance to $1.50 in EPS for the year, putting the company at a P/E of ~10.5x. That's incredibly low for a utility company, especially one that's targeting 9+% in earnings growth for the next 5 years. PG&E is continuing to work with the government to continue its massive capital buildout ($73 billion) with no new equity through 2030.

Additionally, the company is targeting hitting a 20% dividend payout ratio by 2028. For perspective, the company's targeted 2030 EPS is $2.31/share, which implies minimum dividends of $0.46 based on PG&E's payout policy. That's a strong but cheap 3% dividend yield that the company can comfortably afford with a P/E of just over 6x based on your cost basis today.

PG&E Wildfire Policy

The biggest question for PG&E is wildfires as it works to avoid what could be a liability in the $10s of billions.

California is now taking a full state-level approach to handling wildfire risk. From PG&E, that's $10s of billions of capital aiming to solve all incidents and structures. The company has lost no structures in 2023-2025, with 2022 also a substantial improvement. The company has continued to clear vegetation and underground its program along with continuous monitoring.

The California Wildfire Fund was impacted by the early-2025 LA wildfires, but the fund is set up to have an $18 billion continuation account. That will help prevent these utilities from being destroyed by wildfires that increase as climate change becomes worse.

PG&E Data Center

At the same time, PG&E is deploying assets to take advantage of data center demand.

Every GW of data center pipeline is expected to result in a 1-2% electric bill reduction. With almost 10 GWs in the pipeline, that indicates a ~15% electric bill reduction at the midpoint. Only a small portion of this is under construction; however, 18 projects totaling 1.6 GW are under final engineering, which increased by 100 MWs in the last quarter.

While California doesn't have the masses of cheap electricity of other states that will make it data center central, in our view, the utility has enough to focus on right now. East Coast grids are seeing potential massive customer costs from data center assets that have yet to be built, and PG&E needs to focus on being cost competitive to its current customers.

PG&E Investments

PG&E's massive capital program is visible below.

The company is planning on deploying $73 billion in capital over the next 5 years the vast majority in 2028-2030F. PG&E expects to ramp up its rate base as a result of these investments, a substantial percentage of which is already authorized by PG&E. These are one-time mega investments in PG&E's grid that will provide long-term returns.

PG&E is doing this while continuing to invest massively in renewables and electrical storage. In 2023 PG&E gave retail customers 100% carbon-free electricity, and that positions the company well for the long term as it continues to target carbon neutrality for 2040.

PG&E Shareholder Returns

Putting all of these investments together, PG&E is well positioned to generate strong cash flow.

The company is focused on ending equity issuance and focusing on rate base growth and strong returns. It expects load growth to be ~2% annualized and customer bills to be ~3% annualized in line with inflation. We'd like the company to focus on this customer bill component; it has minimal goodwill after the wildfires, and maintaining several years of flat bills would go far.

The company's 2025F dividend payout ratio is only 7%, but operating cash flow is $9 billion with 10% non-GAAP core EPS growth. That's supposed to switch to the high single digits through 2030 as shareholder returns remain high.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is PG&E's ability to continue executing. PG&E has had to deal with some of the highest bills in the country and massive investments needed to mitigate wildfire risk. The company has a plan in place, but it needs years of execution to hit the point where it can drive valuable shareholder returns.

Conclusion

PG&E has an impressive portfolio of assets, but its market capitalization has been crushed to roughly $35 billion. That's because of the multi-year impact of wildfires, with the company ~75% below its 2017 highs. However, PG&E has a plan to underground transmission lines, and already initial work is paying off in risks.

Going forward, PG&E plans to continue expanding its rate base, with the potential to benefit from data centers. PG&E is investing tens of billions, which it expects will lead to lofty shareholder returns. The company has almost $60 billion of debt, which will expand with this capital program, but PG&E will be able to use it to drive strong dividends and shareholder returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: As Copper Prices Soar, Hudbay Minerals Has The Look Of A Winner
data.description:
Back in April, I initiated coverage on Hudbay Minerals (HBM) as a buy, citing the company’s potential for growth and its effort to pay down debt. The stock far exceeded my expectations, as you can see here.

The story of Hudbay’s success tracks most of the mining companies out there. In this case, the price of copper has surged so much that Hudbay’s forward earnings look positively enormous, to the point where even some headwinds that challenged earnings in Q3 and led to a modest miss didn’t impact the stock. The bulls are out in force, and it’s hard to blame them.

We’ll be taking a look at Hudbay as they look today, what happened to them in Q3, and also the status of their various important sites. We’ll also take a look at why the copper market is what it is and whether or not this copper bull market is something we can count on.

Fires And Unrest: Hudbay’s Safe Sites Face Problems

Part of the reason I initially liked Hudbay was that, unlike companies that have mines in seemingly geopolitical “hot zones,” Canada-based Hudbay seemingly has ideal sites. The three major sites included two sites in western Canada and the one in Peru, with a fourth site coming up in Arizona.

Reality quickly showed that safe is a relative term, however. The Constancia site in Peru had some operational interruptions in Q3 because of sporadic social unrest, and the constant wildfires in Manitoba cut back production at the Lalor site about 50%.

Take the underperformance at those two sites and then factor in Copper Mountain in British Columbia coming into the processing of relatively lower-grade ore than normal, and you can see why Hudbay ultimately missed earnings estimates in Q3, despite relatively strong prices.

Weak as Q3 was, 2025 has still been relatively positive for Hudbay, and we’re seeing improved margins in spite of the Q3 slowdown if we look at the full year.

These are strong numbers in and of themselves, but if you factor in the problems Hudbay faced in Q3 and how much higher copper prices are now than they were before, you can see where the company has the potential to make a lot more money than they already are, and considering that, the stock going from $7 per share to around $20 per share might not be unwarranted.

In A Copper Bull Market, Multiples Might Not Be Crazy

Let’s talk numbers here a bit, because when the stock goes up 160%+ in less than a year, sometimes you end up with unsustainably high multiples. But in Hudbay’s case, they were relatively undervalued before, so in some ways it’s not so stark.
